texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract.  Correlations between plasma properties and magnetic fluctuations in  the  subsolar magnetosheath  downstream of  a quasi-perpendicular shock have been found and indicate that mirror  and ion  cyclotronlike fluctuations correlate with  the  magnetosheath proper  and  plasma  depletion layer, respectively (Anderson  and Fuselier, 1993). We  explore  the  entire range  of  magnetic  spectral signatures observed  from the  AMPTE/CCE spacecraft in  the  magnetosheath  downstream of  a  quasi-perpendicular shock.  The magnetic spectral signatures typically progress  from predominantly  compressional  fluctuations, 6Bii/6B ñ  --3, with  F/Fp <  0.2 (F  and  Fp  are the  wave  frequency and  proton gyrofrequency, respectively) to  predominantly transverse fluctuations, 6B /6Bñ-0.3, extending up  to  Fp. The compressional  fluctuations are  characterized by anticorrelation between the  field magnitude and  electron density, n·, and  by  a  small compressibility, C·  --  (·rte/rte)2(B/·Bll) 2 = 0.13,  indicative of  mirror  waves. The spectral characteristics of  the  transverse fluctuations are  in  agreement  with  predictions of  linear Vlasov  theory for  the  H  +  and  He  2+  cyclotron  modes. The power  spectra and local plasma parameters  are  found to  vary  in  concert: mirror waves  occur for/·llp (/·llp · 2tXonpkrllp/B2) =  2, Ap  = rñp/rll p  - 1  --0.4, whereas cyclotron waves  occur for/5'11 p  --0.2 and  Ap  =  2.  The  transition from  mirror to  cyclotron modes  is  predicted by linear theory. The spectral characteristics overlap  for  intermediate plasma parameters. The  plasma observations are described by  Ap  =  0.85/·llp -0'48 with a  log regression coefficient of -0.74. This  inverse Ap  -/·lp correlation corresponds closely to the  isocontours of  maximum  ion  anisotropy instability growth, Ym/·p =  0.01, for the  mirror and cyclotron modes. The agreement of  observed  properties and predictions of  local theory  suggests that  the  spectral signatures reflect the  local plasma environment  and that the  anisotropy instabilities regulate Ap. We  suggest that the  spectral characteristics may provide  a  useful basis  for  ordering  observations in  the  magnetosheath and that  the Ap- ·lp inverse correlation may be  used  as  a  beta-dependent upper  limit on  the  proton anisotropy to  represent kinetic effects.

The phenomenon  is  referred to  as  plasma depletion, and the  region of reduced density  and increased  field  strength  is called  the plasma depletion  layer. Observational  evidence has been reported to support  this  expectation [Paschmann et al.,  1978; Crooker  et al.,  1979]  and  has indicated  that the plasma depletion  layer  preferentially forms when  the upstream  shock  is quasi-perpendicular and  when the magnetosheath field is  northward [Anderson and Fuselier, 1993] (hereinafter referred  to  as  AF).

texts.orig: ULF fluctuations in the magnetosheath  magnetic field indicative of both ion cyclotron  and mirror instabilities have  been  found. Fairfield [1976]  first noted  that the magnetosheath near the magnetopause  exhibited  fluctuations  near 1 Hz, below  the local  proton cyclotron  frequency with  a left-hand transverse nature  suggestive of  ion cyclotron  waves.  Tsurutani et al.  [1982] have  observed large-amplitude compressional  fluctuations, 6B/B = 0.5,  in the  magnetosheath that  were attributed to  the  mirror  instability. Subsequent  authors  also  noted magnetic fluctuations characteristic of the  magnetosheath near  the  magnetopause [Song  et al.,  1990a,  1993; Anderson et al.,  1991; AF].

texts.orig: Anderson  et  al.  [1991] and AF  demonstrated a close  association between the  wave  magnetic fluctuations and plasma properties. They  found that  cyclotron waves occurred  for reduced  plasma  density and  increased magnetic  field strength relative to  values  during  mirror  waves. Their  observations  also suggested that  magnetosheath  ion anisotropies may  be ordered  by the  ion  anisotropy mode  that  is most unstable.

texts.orig: Denton et  al.  [1993]  compared  the  magnetic field spectral  observations  for  one ion cyclotron  wave  event with theoretical predictions using  the  observed  plasma properties and showed  that  the  observed  frequency  and polarization structure of  the  magnetic  fluctuations agree  with  theoretical expectations. The solar wind He  2' was found  to  play  a  significant role  in  determining  the  spectral structure. Gary et al.  [1993a]  used measured  plasma data  to show  that  the observed  transition from mirror  to  cyclotron waves occurs where the  growth rates are  comparable and that  the  mode with  the  larger growth rate is  observed  to  be dominant.

The detectors were oriented to observe particles incident  perpendicular  to the spacecraft spin  axis  so that  two-dimensional  velocity coverage  was provided  by the  spacecraft spin. The spin  period was  6  s,  and  complete energy and species  coverage for ions  required  approximately  2 min  in  the  most favorable instrument  mode.  The  electron spectrometers provided an energy spectrum every  0.155  s.  The spin  axis  was oriented toward the  sun.  In  the  subsolar magnetosheath,  the  X  component of the field is  very small  relative to the Y and Z components. As a result, the  field was within  5  ø  of  the  ion instrument field  of view  in 76 of the 102 intervals, whereas  in  four  cases  the  minimum observed  pitch  angle  was as large  as  30 ø.  In  addition, plasma motion in  the  X direction should  be much  slower  than  the  thermal  speeds  so that  T·_ and  T,  are  reliably determined.

texts.orig: We extend the  observational work of  AF to  consider the  full range  of  spectral features observed  downstream of the quasi-perpendicular shock. Since we  use the Active Magnetospheric  Particle Tracer  Explorers/Charge Composition  Explorer  (AMPTE/CCE) data  set, we  necessarily restrict attention to  the  subsolar magnetosheath for  which the magnetosphere  is  compressed.  AF  was primarily concerned with periods  that  were dominated by either compressional fluctuations or left-hand polarized  transverse waves  above 0.5Fp. In  general, a  continuum of  spectral signatures is  observed  between these  extremes.  Characterizing the  variety of spectral signatures and corresponding  plasma conditions is  the  topic of  this paper.

texts.orig: Section  2 describes the  instrumentation and preliminary data analysis.  Section 3 presents  an example  magnetosheath  pass  that  exhibits the  range of spectral features to be examined.  We  define five  spectral categories on the  basis of  the  observed  morphology. Section  4 presents the  results of statistical analysis  of 102 magnetosheath intervals. The results are  discussed in  terms  of  the  ion  cyclotron and mirror instabilities in section  5.

texts.orig: The AMPTE/CCE spacecraft was in  an equatorial orbit with magnetic latitude in  the  range  +16  ø,  apogee at  8.8RE, perigee  at 1000 km,  and a period  of 15.6  hours.  During normal magnetospheric  conditions the  spacecraft remained within  the magnetosphere throughout  its  orbit, but magnetospheric compressions occasionally pushed the magnetopause  inward  within  the  CCE  apogee.

texts.orig: Plate 1 shows  plasma  and magnetic field  data from 0100  to 0700  UT on  October  6, 1984, illustrating the range of magnetic spectra  that  are  observed in the magnetosheath  downstream  of the  quasi-perpendicular shock. Energetic  solar wind ions  are  a signature of a quasi-parallel  upstream shock [Crooker et  al., 1981; Fuselier  et  al., 1991a;  Kucharek and Scholer,  1991],  so we  use energetic, >10 keV, He  2+  to infer  whether the  upstream shock is quasi-parallel or quasi-perpendicular. The left hand panels show spin-averaged electron number fluxes  from the  67-eV and  8.9-keV channels and  proton density rip together with the  magnetic field in GSM coordinates. Electron  and vector  field  data are  shown with 6-s  resolution. Proton moments were  determined with 2-min resolution. Magnetic fluctuation and proton distribution data  are  shown  in the right-hand panels, which show from top  to  bottom:  ellipticity  of the transverse  fluctuations from  0.03 to 4.0 Hz; transverse power; parallel power; proton  temperature  anisotropy, Ap;  and proton  plasma beta, /5'p-= 2itonpkTp/B 2, Tp  · (2T.c p  +  Tllp)/3. The black  lines indicate the  30-s  averaged  local He  2+ and  H  +  gyrofrequencies, 0.5Fp in  the  second  panel and  Fp  in  the  third panel.

texts.orig: Magnetic field data  are  provided  by the  Magnetic Field Experiment  [Potemra et  al.,  1985],  which  included  a vector  flux  gate  magnetometer system with sensors  mounted on a 2.3-m boom. The experiment  had seven automatically switchable  ranges from +16nT to +65,536 nT  full  scale with 13-bit  resolution  (fib  = 0.004 to 16  nT)  and obtained a vector sample  every 0.124  s.  Data  were  analyzed for ULF  waves in  a local field-aligned coordinate system.

texts.orig: The  spacecraft was  in the magnetosheath from  about 0215  to 0640  UT  as indicated by the low fluxes  of 8.5keV  electrons and high fluxes  of 67-eV  electrons. (Low energy,  50-100 eV, and higher  energy,  5-10 keV  electrons are  characteristic of  the  magnetosheath  and magnetosphere, respectively.) From  0147 to  0215 UT, the  spacecraft stayed near  the  magnetopause,  perhaps  even in  the  diffusion region.  This  is  suggested  by the  variable field direction, high plasma  density, and extraordinarily high  /5'p. Similar fluctuations are commonly observed in association with magnetopause crossings  with high magnetic shear  and have been associated with reconnection processes  [Song et  al., 1993].  We are  interested in  the  magnetosheath  per se,  so we  turn  our attention to the remainder of the magnetosheath  interval.

texts.orig: The variety of  spectral signatures observed  can be organized according  to the  relative intensity of the compressional  signals and the spectral structure of the  transverse power. Prominent compressional  signals occur from 0215 to 0220  UT,  from  0340  to 0400  UT,  and  from  0510  to 0600 UT. Their  frequency is  generally low,  below  0.2Fp, and  they  can  reach  rather large amplitudes, ·Bii/B = 0.3, as shown in  the  B trace. Predominantly  transverse signals also occur: 0220-0240, 0245-0310, 0320-0340, 0400-0440, and  0615-0635 UT.

texts.orig: Figure  1  shows power spectra for  five  intervals of  Plate 1,  one spectrum  for  each category. The time  intervals used to evaluate the  power  spectra are  indicated in  the  panels, and the  plasma parameters  are  given  in  Table 1.  The minimum frequencies of these  spectra, given  by the  reciprocal of the time spans,  are considerably  lower than shown  in the spectrogram  of Plate 1.  The  spectral density  is  displayed  in  left-hand, PL,  right-hand, PR,  and parallel, Pll, components versus frequency normalized to  Fp. To indicate the  frequency  ranges  for  which the  linear Vlasov theory predicts positive ion cyclotron  wave  growth,  the  frequencies for  which  the  ion  cyclotron modes have  ?/·  > 10 -4 are shown  by the horizontal  bars.  The  calculations were performed  using the WHAMP dispersion  solver [see Denton  et  al.,  1993].  Nonlinear and propagation  effects may change  the spectral  structure  somewhat,  but the ranges  of  positive growth generally correspond  to  the  frequencies  of  transverse spectral enhancements.

texts.orig: The  transverse  signals  have  a variety  of frequency ranges and polarizations, and can occur simultaneously with compressional  fluctuations. Transverse  signals below 0.5Fp  are  often linearly polarized, whereas  above  0.5Fp they  are  usually left-hand polarized, as during  0245-0300 UT.  Exceptions  are  found,  however, e.g., 0220-0240  UT (linear above  0.5Fp) and 0530-0600  (left-handed below 0.5Fp). The transverse signals are  limited to  frequencies below Fp.  Transverse  signals below 0.5Fp  are  always present when there are  waves above  0.5Fp  but  not  vice versa;  compare 0400-0420  with 0510-0530  UT.  Spectral slots in  the  transverse power near  0.5Fp  appear occasionally  and are  evident  from 0240 to  0340 UT.  Strong  compressional signals and  transverse waves  with F  >  0.5Fp seem to  be mutually  exclusive; note the  transitions occurring  at 0340 UT  and  0400 UT. Transverse waves with  F  <  0.5Fp do occur with compressional  fluctuations, however, e.g., from 0510 to 0600 UT.

texts.orig: To  provide  a framework  for  quantitative analysis we define  five  spectral categories. The  first category, mirror (MIR), represents instances when  compressional  perturbations  dominate the  spectrum while  there  is  no discernible spectral peak in the transverse power. Note that  the absence  of  a transverse spectral peak does not  mean  that  the transverse power is  negligible. We adopt the  term mirror in  anticipation of the  discussion of wave  modes  presented below. The  second category  consists of intervals with a prominent  compressional peak together with  a  peak at  a  different frequency in  the  transverse spectrum with  F  <  0.5Fp and is  called  MRL for  mirror  with low-frequency  transverse waves. Events  with compressional and transverse peaks at  the same  frequency were considered  to be compressional if  the  compressional  and transverse powers were comparable.  This  situation arose  in  one event. The remaining  categories consist of  intervals with  no discernable peak in  compressional  power  but with  three  different transverse spectral structures: low frequency  (LOW),  transverse waves with  F< 0.5Fv; continuous (CON),  transverse waves  with continuous power  spectra extending above  and  below 0.5F p;  and  bifurcated (B IF), transverse waves with  two broad local maxima  in  spectral power.

The remaining  categories consist of  intervals with  no discernable peak in  compressional  power  but with  three  different transverse spectral structures: low frequency  (LOW),  transverse waves with  F< 0.5Fv; continuous (CON),  transverse waves  with continuous power  spectra extending above  and  below 0.5F p;  and  bifurcated (B IF), transverse waves with  two broad local maxima  in  spectral power.

texts.orig: The frequency  structure of  the  transverse fluctuations is predicted  by  linear  Vlasov  theory of the ion cyclotron modes.  Since  He  2* in  the  magnetosheath is  hotter than  H* [Fusetier et  at., 1991b;  Gary et  at., 1993c],  we  expect  that He  2*  may  be generating cyclotron waves below 0.5Fp. Hence  the  bifurcation feature is  probably  not  analogous  to the spectral gap observed  in  the  magnetosphere,  which is attributed to absorption by cold He*  [e.g., Fraser,  1985]. The fact  we  observe  continuous  spectra also  indicates that the slot  is  not due to absorption  [Denton et  at.,  1993; Gary et  at., 1993b].  The analysis of  Denton et  at.  [1993] shows that He  2*  is  largely responsible for  the  lower  spectral peak and that  protons  drive  waves  with frequencies above  0.5Fp. The polarization structure of  the  transverse fluctuations, linear below  0.5Fp but  left-handed above, can also  be accounted  for  by the  ion  cyclotron modes  [Denton et  at., 1993].

texts.orig: Dynamic  spectra plotted with 2 hours of data  per page were used to identify all  periods  longer  than 5 min  that exhibited  steady  spectral structure. We selected  no  more than one interval from each quarter  hour.  If  the spectral features varied, multiple  intervals were allowed in 15 min since  these  represented independent  measurements of each spectral type.  The  interval durations also  varied  depending on whether the HPCE ion instrument  was  in a rapid  or slow mode. The relative abundance of  spectral types  in  the CCE  data  is  not  reflected in  this analysis.

texts.orig: There is  a mapping  between spectral type and location along  the  Ap- ·llp curve. This  indicates that the  spectral features reflect the  local  plasma conditions, and hence that the  observed  evolution  of spectral signatures results from changes in the  plasma. In addition, it  was  shown  in AF that  the BIF  and CON categories  are indicative of the plasma depletion layer, whereas the  MIR category  corresponds to the magnetosheath proper.  Since  the  opposite ends  of  the Ap-/·llp curve represent extremes in  spatial location, the  inverse correlation suggests that the  plasma follows  a well-defined path  in  Ap-/·llp space as  it convects from the  bow  shock toward the  magnetopause.

texts.orig: Linear  Vlasov theory  predicts that  for  the  anisotropic, T·.> Tll, H+  and  He 2+  of  the  magnetosheath, three distinct growing modes  may  arise: mirror, proton  cyclotron anisotropy,  and helium cyclotron  anisotropy instabilities [Gary et  al., 1993a]. The mirror  instability has zero  real frequency in  the  plasma center-of-mass frame,  propagates  with  wave vector  substantially oblique to the background  magnetic field B, and has a fluctuating magnetic  field predominantly in the  direction parallel to  B  [Gary,  1992,  and references therein]. The  two cyclotron  instabilities have nonzero real frequencies bounded  above by their  respective cyclotron frequencies, maximum growth rates at  wave  vectors parallel to  B,  and fluctuating magnetic  fields predominantly  perpendicular to  B  [Gary et  al,  1993a;  Denton  et  al., 1993]. That the  cyclotron modes  are  responsible for  the  transverse fluctuations is  indicted by the  agreement  between the  predicted  and observed  transverse spectral structures. With regard to  the  compressional  fluctuations, detailed correlation analysis of the density  and field perturbations implicates the  mirror  mode.

texts.orig: We  suggest that the  evolution of  spectral features may result naturally from  the behavior of  the mirror and  cyclotron modes  along the  observed Ap-/·lIp relationship. For /·,· <<  1  (Ap >>  1), linear theory predicts that the cyclotron instabilities have  the  lower anisotropy threshold [Gary  et al., 1993a]  and that the  proton  and helium  cyclotron modes  grow  in  distinct frequency ranges, the proton mode growing for F> 0.5Fp and  the helium mode  below 0.5Fp [Denton et  al., 1993]. This corresponds to  the BIF  spectra that occur for/·,· <<  1. For  slightly higher/·11p (lower Ap), the frequencies ofmaximum growth rate of  the proton and helium  cyclotron instabilities d crease, but  the  proton mode frequency decreases more,  leading to  an  overlap of the  frequency regimes of  the  two cyclotron modes and  the production of  a single, continuous spectral peak.  The plasma  regime of  overlapping proton and  He 2*  generated waves corresponds in  Ap-/·llp space  to  the  loci of  the CON  spectra. As /·llp (Ap) increases (decreases) further, the proton cyclotron mode  frequency decreases below 0.5Fp, leaving waves  only below  0.5Fp, and  this regime is populated by LOW spectra. Since  the  He  2*  and H* mode  frequencies overlap, one  cannot tell from  the frequency structure which  cyclotron mode is  responsible for  the  single peak  below  0.5Fp. As /·llp (Ap) increases (decreases) yet further, the  maximum growth rate of  the proton cyclotron instability falls close  to  or  below that of the  mirror  mode [Gary  et  al., 1993a]  and  the  two proton  modes become competitive. Meanwhile,  the  He  :*  cyclotron mode remains unstable. The simultaneous occurrence of  mirror and  cyclotron modes  leads to the MRL spectra. Finally, for/·,p>> 1 (Ap  less than  about 0.4), the  mirror instability growth  rate becomes larger than  both  cyclotron modes and dominates the  spectral power  giving MIR  spectra. v

texts.orig: The simulations of  McKean et  al.  [1992]  and Gary and Winske [1993]  are  initial value  computations  that  require relatively large  start-up anisotropies and therefore do not necessarily correspond  to  the  observed  magnetosheath  conditions. The "refreshed" simulations of  Denton  et  al.  [1993] are  probably  a more appropriate way  to  address  the  model parameters,  since  the actual  state  of the plasma  results from a dynamic  equilibrium  between plasma convection, which drives the  anisotropy up,  and the  waves that reduce it. Preliminary results from the initial value  computations provide  reason  for  optimism that  these  aspects of the  observations will  also  find  direct explanation in  terms  of  ion anisotropy instabilities.

texts.orig: We  have characterized the  magnetic spectral signatures and plasma properties observed  in  the  subsolar magnetosheath  for  quasi-perpendicular upstream shock conditions. The  magnetic spectral signatures and plasma properties were  found  to  progress from  mirror waves  with  high  f·llp and  low  Ap  in  the  magnetosheath proper to  H  +  and  He  2+ cyclotron waves  associated with high Ap  and  low  ·llp in  the plasma depletion layer.
texts.text: We  have characterized the  magnetic spectral signatures and plasma properties observed  in  the  subsolar magnetosheath  for  quasi-perpendicular upstream shock conditions. The  magnetic spectral signatures and plasma properties were  found  to  progress from  mirror waves  with  high  f·llp and  low  Ap  in  the  magnetosheath proper to  H  +  and  He  2+ cyclotron waves  associated with high Ap  and  low  ·llp in  the plasma depletion layer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Linear  Vlasov  theory has  been  used  to  test the  interpretation that the  mirror  and cyclotron modes  driven  by the ion temperature  anisotropies are  responsible for  the observed  spectral features [Gary et  al., 1993a;  Denton et  al., 1993]. Linear  theory  correctly predicts which modes should be observed  and also  appears to  predict the  spectral structure  of the  cyclotron  waves. Furthermore,  simulations of polarization and saturation amplitudes  of the  mirror  and cyclotron modes  indicate that  these  features also  arise from the dynamics of these modes [McKean et al.,  1992; Denton  et  al., 1993; Gary  and  Winske, 1993].  We conclude  that the  evolution of spectral structures may  readily be explained by the  generation of  mirror  and cyclotron instabilities in  the  plasma as  it convects  earthward  from the shock  to  the  magnetopause.

texts.orig: The agreement between observed spectral structure and predicted frequency  ranges  of  positive growth presented  in Figure 1 is  tantalizing. It  is  important  to  assess  how  well the  linear Vlasov theory  predicts the  evolution of spectral structures more generally, perhaps using the  Ap-/·llp relation. If  linear theory  continues  to  be successful, this  will substantiate the  mode identifications and the  conclusion that  the  waves are  generated  locally. This  theoretical study is  under  way.

texts.orig: Theoretical studies of the  fluctuation amplitudes, polarization structure, and further relationships among  the  plasma parameters for  comparison  with  the  observations are  of  considerable  interest but  are  more difficult.  As Denton et al. [1993]  have discussed, treatment  of these  issues  requires that  nonlinear saturation effects  be included,  which  in turn implies  that  attention must be paid to the  timescales  of wave  growth and residence  with resonant  particles. Hence quantitative predictions concerning  spectral amplitudes  and plasma  responses to instability growth  may  come  only from fully nonlinear computer simulations.

Density  and field correlations show both that  the  field and density perturbations  are  in  antiphase, and that  the  compressibility C· is small, 0.13  on average, consistent with  the  expectation that C·  < 1  for  mirror  waves.
texts.text: Additional evidence  that  the  compressional fluctuations result from the mirror  mode  is  provided by correlation analysis of  density and field fluctuations. Density  and field correlations show both that  the  field and density perturbations  are  in  antiphase, and that  the  compressibility C· is small, 0.13  on average, consistent with  the  expectation that C·  < 1  for  mirror  waves.

texts.orig: The widespread  generation of ion  anisotropy modes  in the  subsolar magnetosheath  plasma implies that  wave-particle interactions play  a role  in  determining the  ion  anisotropy  state of  the  plasma.  This  is  clearly indicated by Figure  6.  Since  the  ions  carry  the  majority of  plasma  pressure in  the  magnetosheath, any process  that  regulates the  distribution  of ion  pressures  may  be significant for  magnetosheath  dynamics.  To  accurately model the  magnetosheath, proper account of the effects of the locally  generated anisotropy instabilities needs  to  be made. We  suggest  that this include wave-particle ffects using  the  relationship between  Ap and /·llp as  an the  upper  limit on the  proton anisotropy.
